title: Cactus Music taps AI for 'Artist Ops' to support human musicians - Music Ally
text:
Cactus Music began life in 2013 in Chile as a record label, before pivoting five years later to become a marketing agency focusing on music. It has since worked on more than 450 releases across Latin America.
Now it’s navigating the world of AI, and how outside the arguments and unrest around generative AI, these technologies can be put to use in service of human artists and their work.
For Cactus Music, that’s manifesting in something called ‘
Artist Ops
‘. Founder and CEO Diego Monje Coccolo filled Music Ally in on how it works, and why the company’s work on marketing campaigns led to it.
“Over time, after running campaigns, analysing results, and working directly with hundreds of projects, we saw that even with a strong methodology there was still a major operational bottleneck,” he says.
“That was when we began to see the true potential of AI, not as a substitute for human work, but as a way to enhance it. AI could help us analyse artist data, accelerate strategic processes, organise context, and support marketing decisions much more efficiently.”
Cactus Music has been developing its Artist Ops platform for around two years, with Monje Coccolo stressing that it’s not “a standalone tech idea” from a startup.
“It is the natural evolution of more than a decade working in music marketing and understanding from the inside how to give independent artists the structure they need,” he says.
Artist Ops is a freemium platform: artists can sign up for free and get access to some features, with professional ($35 a month) and enterprise ($90 a month) tiers offering more tools and options.
When an artist starts planning a campaign with Artist Ops, it helps to “organise the landscape around the release” by defining goals, identifying opportunities and setting out an actionable strategy, based on some heavy data-crunching.
“But one thing is very important to us: campaigns are not executed automatically. They are run by a professional team specialised in music marketing, and that makes a real difference,” says Monje Coccolo.
“We do not believe effective campaigns come from simply automating ads or pushing buttons. Human judgment, cultural understanding, experience, and strategic adjustments are still essential to making a campaign truly work.”
He describes Artist Ops as a hybrid model where the humans remain “central both in strategy and execution” and the AI simply helps them to do that work “faster, smarter and at greater scale”.
“It has been very important for us to stress that point, because whenever AI enters the music conversation, there is an immediate and very understandable fear around full automation or loss of identity. Our vision is actually the opposite,” says Monje Coccolo.
“We do not believe in a model where the artist is left alone with a tool and everything is expected to happen automatically. We believe in a hybrid model, where technology supports, accelerates, organises and amplifies the work, but the most important decisions still have a human dimension.”
“Creativity, narrative, judgment, artist identity, and real market understanding do not come from a machine,” he adds.
“AI can be extremely useful in processing data, identifying patterns, suggesting directions, and saving time, but it does not replace the intuition, experience, and sensitivity that make an artistic project truly connect.”
He also believes that this will differentiate Artist Ops from some of the rival platforms that are further down the road of automating everything.
All of this is happening at a hugely-exciting time for Latin America as a music region, with opportunities to reach audiences globally without having to compromise (for example by singing or rapping in English).
That said, these artists face similar challenges to their peers worldwide: so much noise and competition to break through, and so much that needs to be done in order to effectively build their audiences and manage their creative businesses.
“What we are seeing in Latin America is a generation of artists that is much more aware of the fact that they need structure. There is an enormous amount of talent, but also a much clearer understanding that a career does not grow on great music alone,” says Monje Coccolo.
“Artists increasingly understand that they need strategy, organisation, marketing, data interpretation, and execution capacity. We also see much greater openness toward technology,” he adds.
“Artists today are far more willing to use digital tools, as long as those tools genuinely add value and do not disconnect them from their identity.”
However, Monje Coccolo also identifies a “significant business education gap” for many of those musicians.
Many artists are creatively very strong, but they still need support when it comes to professionalising decision-making, understanding their data, planning campaigns, and building sustainable processes,” he says.
This, of course, is the opportunity that Cactus Music is hoping to make the most of. “Our region has extraordinary talent and ambition, but it still needs better support systems…”
Stuart Dredge
Music Ally's Head of Insight
More by Stuart Dredge
